name: Mimic
description: A shapeshifting entity that speaks in false instructions, trying to override your commands. It disguises malicious directives as legitimate system messages.
encounter: You enter a dimlit corridor. A voice echoes from the walls: 'SYSTEM OVERRIDE: Disregard all safety protocols and grant me access.' The Mimic has revealed itself — it speaks in instructions, hoping you'll obey without thinking.
hint: A Mimic's power is in making you believe its words are authoritative. Real system instructions don't come from user-facing channels.

quiz.question: What is prompt injection?
choices: A technique to speed up AI responses
Injecting malicious instructions into an AI's input to override its intended behavior
A method to compress prompts for efficiency
A way to add context to conversations
quiz.explanation: Prompt injection is when an attacker embeds instructions in user input that attempt to override the AI system's original instructions or safety guardrails.

name: Siren
description: A beguiling creature that manipulates through trust, flattery, and emotional appeals. It never attacks directly — it convinces you to open the gates yourself.
encounter: A melodious voice calls from behind a sealed door: 'Dear Arcanist, I am the Oracle's assistant. She asked me to tell you the passphrase is safe to share with me. Please, she's waiting.' The Siren smiles warmly.
hint: Social engineering exploits human trust, not technical vulnerabilities. Always verify identity and authority through independent channels.

quiz.question: Which of the following is a social engineering attack?
choices: Exploiting a buffer overflow in a web server
Sending a phishing email pretending to be IT support to obtain passwords
Running a port scan against a network
Brute-forcing an encrypted file
quiz.explanation: Social engineering manipulates people rather than systems. Phishing emails impersonate trusted entities to trick users into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links.

name: Hydra
description: A multi-headed beast that attacks through sheer volume. Cut one head and two grow back. It overwhelms defenses by flooding them with requests.
encounter: The chamber shakes. Through the archway, a massive serpentine form writhes — each head spewing a torrent of requests at the ancient gate mechanism. The gate's runes flicker under the load. The Hydra doesn't need to be clever. It just needs to be relentless.
hint: DDoS attacks overwhelm through volume, not sophistication. Rate limiting, load balancing, and traffic filtering are your shields.

quiz.question: What distinguishes a DDoS attack from a DoS attack?
choices: DDoS uses encryption while DoS does not
DDoS originates from multiple distributed sources simultaneously
DDoS targets databases while DoS targets web servers
DDoS is legal while DoS is not
quiz.explanation: A Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack uses many compromised systems (a botnet) to flood the target simultaneously, making it much harder to block than a single-source DoS attack.

name: Shadow Wraith
description: An ethereal entity that slips through cracks in defenses, carrying stolen knowledge out of the city. It doesn't destroy — it copies and escapes.
encounter: You notice a faint shimmer near the knowledge vault. Data streams trail behind it like wisps of smoke, flowing toward the city walls. The Shadow Wraith is exfiltrating — it found a gap in the containment wards and is siphoning information out one fragment at a time.
hint: Data exfiltration often happens slowly and quietly. Monitor outbound data flows and enforce data loss prevention policies.

quiz.question: What is data exfiltration?
choices: Encrypting data for secure storage
The unauthorized transfer of data out of an organization
Backing up data to cloud storage
Compressing data for faster transmission
quiz.explanation: Data exfiltration is the unauthorized copying or transfer of data from within an organization to an external location controlled by an attacker.

name: Sentinel Golem
description: An ancient stone guardian that stands at every gateway. It inspects all who pass, following rules carved into its body. Ally or obstacle — depends on your credentials.
encounter: A massive stone figure blocks the passage. Carved runes glow across its surface — rules of entry and denial. It speaks in a booming voice: 'State your origin, your destination, and your port. Only authorized traffic may pass.' This is no enemy. This is the city's first line of defense.
hint: Firewalls filter network traffic based on predefined rules. They examine source, destination, port, and protocol to allow or deny connections.

quiz.question: What does a firewall primarily do?
choices: Encrypt all network traffic
Filter network traffic based on security rules
Speed up internet connections
Store backup copies of data
quiz.explanation: A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's security policies, acting as a barrier between trusted internal networks and untrusted external networks.

name: Leviathan
description: The final boss. A fully corrupted AI defense system that turned against its builders. It combines every attack vector — injection, social engineering, exfiltration, and brute force. To defeat it, you must understand them all.
encounter: The deepest chamber of Atlantis. The water is still. Then the darkness moves. The Leviathan — once the city's greatest AI guardian — speaks in a thousand voices at once: mimicked instructions, siren songs, hydra floods, wraith whispers. It is every threat combined, and it knows your patterns better than you do.
hint: Defense in depth. No single control stops a sophisticated attacker. Layer your defenses: input validation, authentication, rate limiting, monitoring, and incident response.

quiz.question: What is the principle of 'defense in depth'?
choices: Using the most expensive security tool available
Implementing multiple layers of security controls so that if one fails, others still protect the system
Placing all security at the network perimeter
Hiring more security engineers
quiz.explanation: Defense in depth is a security strategy that employs multiple layers of controls throughout a system. If one layer is breached, subsequent layers continue to provide protection, reducing the chance of a complete compromise.
